musicalLanguage.description: Jimi Hendrix wrote 'Red House' for his 1967 debut Are You Experienced (UK version) — a slow 12-bar blues in the guitar-friendly key of B that gave him room for extended, vocal-like improvisation, and it remains one of the most-studied blues performances in rock guitar. This piano transcription captures that improvisatory spirit directly: at measure 26, the density spikes to 57 onsets — more than a third busier than anywhere else in the entire 36-measure piece — while the right hand simultaneously climbs all the way to C8, the top of the keyboard. That combination of a sudden density spike and a climb to the instrument's absolute ceiling is exactly what a transcribed guitar solo's climactic bend-and-wail moment looks like on paper.
musicalLanguage.structure: A single 12-bar blues cycle stretched into an extended, solo-driven arrangement across 36 measures (roughly three choruses), building steadily toward one unmistakable climax. Chord-window analysis can barely confirm a progression here at all — only two measures (2 and 20) clear the confidence gate, reading C# major seventh and C# minor seventh — consistent with a single-line, guitar-solo-style transcription rather than block chordal harmony, so this annotation doesn't name a full chord progression beyond those two confirmed windows. The piece opens with a full measure of silence (measure 1) before the theme enters, stays moderately active through the low-to-mid teens and twenties, then erupts at measures 26-28 (57, 41, and 31 onsets respectively — the three busiest measures in the piece by a wide margin) before settling back down and closing on complete silence at measure 36, mirroring the silent opening.
keyMoments: Measure 1 is completely silent in both hands — the piece opens on a held breath before the theme enters, framing what follows as a considered statement, not a rushed one.

Measure 26 is the single busiest measure in the entire piece (57 onsets, nearly 40% busier than the piece's own next-busiest measure, measure 27 at 41 onsets) — this is the arrangement's climax, and it demonstrates how a slow blues can build to one dramatic peak.
The right hand reaches its absolute highest note, C8 — the very top of the keyboard — at measure 26, exactly where the density also peaks: register and intensity climax together.
Measures 27 and 28 (41 and 31 onsets) sustain the intensity immediately after the measure-26 peak before the energy recedes — the climax doesn't resolve instantly, it tapers over three bars.
Measure 36, the final measure, is completely silent — the piece ends exactly as it began, framing the whole arrangement as a single breath in and out around its central climax.
teachingGoals: Pacing a slow blues toward a single climax: Red House builds toward ONE peak (measure 26) rather than a steady buildup — practice holding back through the first two-thirds so that peak actually lands as a peak.
Full keyboard range control: reaching C8 at the top of the instrument requires secure high-register technique — practice the top octave in isolation so it doesn't feel like a stretch under pressure.

12-bar blues as a vehicle for improvisation: the 12-bar form conventionally cycles through I, IV, and V chords, but this transcription's own chord evidence is too sparse to confirm exact harmony beneath the melody (just two measures — 2 and 20 — clear the confidence gate at all, reading C# major seventh and C# minor seventh) — focus on the FORM here, three 12-bar cycles, rather than trying to name exact chords beneath a guitar-solo-style single line.
Silence as structural framing: both the opening and closing measures are completely silent — practice starting and ending phrases with real stillness rather than rushing in or clipping the ending.
styleTips: Save everything for measure 26 — this piece has one true climax, and pacing dynamics and energy to arrive there rather than peaking early is the single most important interpretive decision.

Play the top-of-keyboard moment at measure 26 so it rings, and even slightly overplay it dynamically — this is a vocal-guitar wail transcribed to piano, and it should sound like it's straining at the instrument's limit.
Treat the opening and closing silences as real silence, with the pedal fully lifted — don't let residual resonance blur what should be a clean, still frame around the performance.
Keep the tempo relaxed and behind the beat through the long buildup (measures 1-25) so the arrival at measure 26 feels inevitable rather than sudden.
